skill.name: remotion-best-practices
skill.description: Guidance for building Remotion videos with React, including compositions, media assets, animation timing, captions, fonts, and rendering patterns.
skill.summary: Apply practical Remotion patterns for React-based video projects.
skill.author: remotion-dev

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the high labels are false positives from Markdown front matter, inline code, fenced examples, and TypeScript template literals. The skill is documentation for Remotion and does not include executable setup scripts or hidden automation. Low risk remains because users may copy package installation commands or examples that fetch remote media and data.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives From Documentation Syntax
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution and weak cryptographic algorithm findings are not executable code. Reviewed examples show Markdown code spans, fenced TypeScript or bash snippets, front matter descriptions, and template literals rather than hidden command execution or cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Sampled flagged locations are clearly Markdown metadata, Markdown code fences, TypeScript template literals, or relative imports. I found no evidence of executable shell dispatch, weak cryptography, or filesystem traversal in the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Package Installation Commands In Examples
low_findings.description: Several rule files tell users to run package manager commands such as npx, bunx, yarn, or pnpm. This is expected Remotion setup guidance, but users should review dependency changes before running copied commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible setup instructions for official Remotion packages and are not executed by the skill. Risk is limited to user action after copying commands into a local project.

low_findings.title: Remote Fetch And Media URL Examples
low_findings.description: The documentation includes examples for remote media URLs and fetch calls using user-provided or example endpoints. This is legitimate for Remotion metadata and asset workflows, but copied code should validate trusted URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network usage appears in instructional examples and is aligned with media-loading use cases. There is no evidence of credential exfiltration or hidden outbound requests.

content.value_statement: Remotion projects can become hard to structure when media, timing, captions, and metadata interact. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical rules for building reliable React video compositions.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to define Remotion compositions, stills, folders, default props, and dynamic metadata.
Guides asset usage for images, videos, audio, fonts, GIFs, Lottie files, and remote media URLs.
Provides animation patterns for frame-based timing, springs, transitions, sequencing, trimming, and text effects.
Covers captions, SRT parsing, transcription options, and word-level caption display patterns.
Documents media inspection workflows for duration, dimensions, decoding checks, and frame extraction.
Includes reusable example components for charts and text animations.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete Remotion application scaffold.
It does not verify that copied remote URLs or third-party assets are trusted.
It does not run renders, benchmark performance, or inspect generated video output.
It assumes the target project already uses Remotion and React.

use_cases.title: Structure a Remotion project
use_cases.description: Use the rules to organize compositions, props, folders, assets, and dynamic metadata before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Improve motion and timing
use_cases.description: Apply frame-driven animation, spring, transition, sequence, trim, and text animation patterns to make renders deterministic.

use_cases.title: Handle media and captions
use_cases.description: Reference audio, video, images, fonts, GIFs, Lottie files, and captions with Remotion-specific loading patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Review my Remotion component
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Remotion component for best practices. Focus on assets, timing, composition props, and frame-driven animation.

prompt_templates.title: Add media safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add images, video, audio, and fonts to this Remotion project. Use the recommended Remotion components and loading patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Design a timed sequence
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Remotion sequence with transitions, trimmed clips, captions, and text animations. Explain the frame timing before writing code.

prompt_templates.title: Debug dynamic metadata
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this calculateMetadata flow and media inspection logic. Find issues with duration, dimensions, props, fetch cancellation, and render determinism.

output_examples.input: A user asks why a Remotion animation flickers during rendering.
output_examples.output: The response identifies non-frame-driven animation as the likely cause and recommends using the current frame for deterministic motion.

output_examples.input: A user wants captions from an existing subtitle file.
output_examples.output: The response explains loading the SRT from the public folder, parsing it, delaying render while loading, and displaying parsed captions.

output_examples.input: A user needs a video duration to set composition length.
output_examples.output: The response recommends reading media metadata before rendering and converting seconds to frames using the composition frame rate.

best_practices: Drive animation from Remotion frame state so previews and renders match.
Use Remotion asset helpers and media components so resources load before rendering.
Validate remote media and data sources before copying fetch-based examples into production.
anti_patterns: Using browser time, CSS transitions, or autonomous animation loops for render-critical motion.
Referencing public assets with raw relative paths instead of Remotion asset helpers.
Fetching untrusted remote URLs without cancellation, validation, or error handling.

faq.question: Does this skill create a full Remotion project?
faq.answer: No. It provides best-practice guidance and examples for existing Remotion and React projects.

faq.question: Can it help with captions?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers SRT parsing, caption display patterns, and transcription options for Remotion workflows.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Does it run package installation commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It only documents package manager commands that users may choose to run.

faq.question: Are remote media examples included?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include remote URLs and fetch calls, so production code should allow only trusted sources.

faq.question: Is this security audit blocked?
faq.answer: No. The audit marks the skill as low risk and safe to publish with normal user caution.
